summary: George Hotz argues that online dating apps are fundamentally broken due to misaligned business models and poor user experience design. He proposes three major fixes: charge men more to balance gender ratios (citing Seeking's 4:1 female-to-male ratio as proof), de-emphasize pictures in favor of compatibility signals and linked social media accounts, and redesign the apps to function like CRM software with lead qualification metrics. He suggests these changes could be implemented as a third-party overlay on existing platforms, scraping their data to create a better experience.
key_points: Dating apps should charge men significantly more than women to fix the gender ratio problem - Tinder is 75% male while Seeking's $109/month male fee creates a 4:1 female ratio
Pictures poorly predict date quality; compatibility questions and linked social media (Instagram for looks, Twitter for personality) would be more honest and effective
Dating should be treated like sales with CRM-style interfaces: find leads, qualify them, track meet-to-conversation ratios publicly
The Nash equilibrium of radical transparency means nobody gains an edge from hiding information, so everyone should just accept honesty
A third-party CRM overlay scraping existing dating apps could disrupt the industry without platform approval
money_quotes: First off, you need a business model that isn't mobile gaming.
Charge men money per month. Only men. Every club promoter understands this.

With almost no exceptions, they are fairly similar looks wise. The bottom date the bottom, the middle date the middle, and the most attractive date the most attractive. You will end up dating your looks match.
The dating cycle is the same thing as the sales cycle. Find leads, connect, qualify leads, present, overcome objections, close the deal, and nurture.
If you aren't agreeing on a time to meet in the first ten messages, this is a dead lead.
The Nash equilibrium is that nobody will be able to get an edge on anyone else by hiding things, and the sooner everyone accepts that the sooner we can all have a nice experience.
Everyone would have a better time. Except the shit online dating companies, which need to be disrupted.
key_insight: Online dating apps are broken because they optimize for engagement rather than matches; fixing them requires charging men more, emphasizing compatibility over photos, and treating dating like a CRM sales pipeline with transparent success metrics.
